parts.name: Valve Cover

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pulling the valve cover reveals carbon-fouled plugs you couldn't see from outside. Classic.

flavorText: Aluminum cover's got a hairline crack — probably why it's been weeping oil onto the headers.
The gasket's so crusty it basically fell off in your hand. Forty years of neglect right there.
Someone cross-threaded two of the bolts. Of course they did.

parts.name: Spark Plugs
flavorText: Plugs are black and sooty — classic rich mixture. Either the injectors are off or someone never let this thing breathe.
The electrode gap is twice what it should be. These haven't been touched in years.
One plug has a cracked porcelain tip. That explains a lot, actually.

parts.name: Cam Gears

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Cam gears are off — and now you can see the timing belt clearly. That thing is cracked and glazed all the way around.

flavorText: The vernier marks are maxed out on the exhaust cam. Someone was chasing power and never set it back.
Both gears turn freely without the belt — good sign, no binding in the cams.
Found a stress crack on the intake gear flange. Rare on these, but after forty years, not impossible.

parts.name: Timing Belt

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Belt's off. Behind it, the crank seal is seeping oil — small puddle at the bottom of the cover says this has been going on a while.

flavorText: Cracks across every tooth on the inside. This was maybe one heat cycle from snapping.
No date stamp anywhere on it. Could be original from '85. Could be from last year. Who knows.
Previous owner at least put in an OEM-spec belt. Points for that, nothing else.

parts.name: Head Gasket
flavorText: Water jacket seeping into cylinder two. The coolant is a beautiful shade of caramel. Not good.
Compression leak between cylinders three and four — you can feel it blowing back on the tester.
Gasket looks fine until you check the fire ring on cylinder one. Blown. Barely, but blown.

parts.name: Crankshaft Front Seal
flavorText: Rubber's hardened and cracked. Oil's been painting the front of the block for a while.
Seal lip is completely flat — no spring tension left at all. It's just a ring of hard plastic now.
Cheap fix, genuinely satisfying. New seal goes in, oil stays where it belongs.

parts.name: Oil Pan

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pan drops and there's a fine layer of metallic glitter settled at the bottom. The oil pump screen is going to need a look.

flavorText: Drain plug's been overtightened so many times the threads are stripped. Drain plug repair kit time.
Pan has a small dent from a speed bump encounter. Didn't crack it, but it's close.
Gasket is a compressed paper wafer. Remove it without sneezing or it disintegrates in your hands.

parts.name: Oil Pump
flavorText: Pump gears have light scoring — it's been running low on oil at some point. Functional, but on borrowed time.
Relief valve is stuck slightly open. Oil pressure at idle has been lower than spec for years.
Screen's clogged with sludge. Clean it out or replace the whole unit — your call.

parts.name: Intake Manifold

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Manifold comes off and there's a vacuum line behind it that's been pinched between the block and the flange. Found your idle surge.

flavorText: Runner walls are coated in thick varnish — previous owner ran cheap fuel for years.
Two of the mounting studs are bent. Whoever torqued this down was having a very bad day.
Port matching actually looks decent. Someone did real work on this car at some point.

parts.name: Throttle Body
flavorText: Butterfly plate has carbon deposits thick enough to reduce airflow by ten percent easy.
TPS reads 4.6V at wide-open throttle instead of 5V. Someone adjusted it wrong and left it.
Bore is scored from years of abrasive air. Still functional, but a new one will feel night-and-day.

parts.name: Idle-Up Valve
flavorText: Valve's stuck open — idle drops to 400 RPM when it should rise. Stalls in traffic with the AC on.
Solenoid's dead. No click, no movement. Someone bypassed it with a zip tie at some point.
Works fine when cold, hunts at operating temp. Classic failing diaphragm on these.

parts.name: Vacuum Lines
flavorText: At least three are cracked at the fittings. The whole loom needs replacing — just buy the kit.
Someone replaced them with the wrong inner diameter hose. Held on by prayer and friction.
The brake booster line has a pinhole. That's why the brakes feel wooden with the engine running.

parts.name: Fuel Pump
flavorText: Pump whines loud enough to hear in the cabin. Still building pressure, but it won't last the month.
No prime pulse when you turn the key to ON. Tank's not the problem — pump's dead.
Armature brushes worn to nothing. Thirty-year-old pump. Impressive it lasted this long.

parts.name: Fuel Filter

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Filter was so clogged it was starving the rail. With it gone you can now see the regulator is weeping fuel from the diaphragm too.

flavorText: Cut it open and it's basically a solid block of rust and debris. Amazing anything was getting through.
Filter's only two years old by the sticker, but the tank must be rusty — it's completely saturated.
This is the cheap fix that might be the whole problem. Replace this first before chasing gremlins.

parts.name: Fuel Injectors

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Injectors out. The fuel rail has a micro-crack at the end cap weld — no wonder fuel pressure has been dropping at shutdown.

flavorText: Injector two is completely seized — no spray pattern at all. Dead cylinder has been dragging the engine for who knows how long.
Flow rate on all four is all over the place. Cylinders running rich and lean in the same firing order.
O-rings have shrunk and hardened. You can smell fuel from two feet away. That's a fire hazard.

parts.name: Fuel Pressure Regulator
flavorText: Diaphragm is leaking fuel directly into the vacuum port. You can smell it in the intake.
Regulator reads 42 PSI at idle instead of 36 — injectors have been over-fueling the whole time.
Rail pressure drops to zero in under a minute after shutdown. It's leaking back through the regulator.

parts.name: Fuel Rail
flavorText: End cap weld has a hairline crack — visible as a dark stain on the aluminum. Replace it.
Rail itself is fine but whoever installed it last stripped the banjo bolt threads. Classic.
Pressure test shows it holds — barely. For a fuel system, barely isn't good enough.

parts.name: Radiator

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Radiator's out. The lower hose has a blister on it that's about to let go — you'd have seen that in about 500 more miles.

flavorText: Lower tank is cracked and has been JB Welded. Twice. The second repair is already failing.
Fins are about forty percent collapsed from road debris. Cooling efficiency is way down.
Core looks okay but the inlet neck is corroded through. Coolant loss was slow enough nobody noticed.

parts.name: Thermostat
flavorText: Stuck closed — engine hits redline on the temp gauge in ten minutes of idle. Scary symptom, simple fix.
Stuck open — engine never reaches operating temp. Poor economy, rich running, never fully warm.
Jiggle valve is missing so there's a permanent airlock. Looks fine until it suddenly isn't.

parts.name: Water Pump
flavorText: Bearing is growling — you can feel it through the belt. Not today's problem, but soon.
Impeller vanes are completely corroded away. It's spinning. Nothing is being pumped. Zero flow.
Weep hole is dripping — seal is gone. It's been slowly dumping coolant into the timing belt area.

parts.name: Coolant Hoses
flavorText: Squeeze any of them and they feel like cardboard. The rubber's fully hardened — replace all four.
Lower hose has a blister near the water pump. It's going to blow, question is just when.
Hose clamps are all rusted and two are cracked. Replace hoses and clamps while you're already in there.

parts.name: Radiator Cap
flavorText: Pressure valve releases at 0.3 bar instead of 0.9. System can't hold pressure — coolant boils early.
Rubber seal is deformed and won't seat. You can pull it off without any resistance. That's not right.
Cheapest fix on the car. Throw a new cap on before you condemn anything else in the cooling system.

parts.name: Exhaust Header
flavorText: Collector weld has a crack — you can hear it as a tick at idle that disappears above 2000 RPM.
Someone put an aftermarket four-into-one on here. Not complaining — it's actually a decent unit.
Primaries are coated with rust scale inside. Flow's restricted but it's not cracked anywhere.

parts.name: Catalytic Converter
flavorText: Shake it and the substrate rattles loose inside. It's basically gravel in a can at this point.
Someone drilled it out. Well, the airflow is great. Emissions test, not so much.
Cat is fine. For once, something is fine. Move along.

parts.name: Muffler
flavorText: Rusty and half-perforated. The neighbors have been very patient.
Aftermarket unit — loudest muffler ever made. Previous owner had strong opinions.
Packed with steel wool that's completely disintegrated. It's essentially an open pipe now.

parts.name: O2 Sensor
flavorText: Sensor reads 0.1V flat — stuck lean signal. ECU's been dumping extra fuel trying to compensate.
Response time is 800ms. Should be under 300. Not dead, just very slow. Fuel correction is sluggish.
Connector corroded green and snapped apart when you touched it. New sensor comes with a new connector anyway.

parts.name: Clutch Disc

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Disc is off. Pressure plate fingers are uneven — someone's been riding the clutch for years. Both go together.

flavorText: Friction material worn down to the rivets. It's been slipping under load for a while.
Torsional springs on the hub are broken — that clunk when you let out the clutch is this, not the gearbox.
Glazed from holding it on a hill with the clutch. Not burned, just glass-smooth.

parts.name: Pressure Plate

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pressure plate off. Flywheel face has a heat crack running through it. Step three of the clutch job just got more expensive.

flavorText: Three of the diaphragm spring fingers are cracked at the base. Clutch engagement has been unpredictable.
Pressure plate face is grooved and blued from heat. It's been through it.
Hardware kit comes with new bolts — use them. Reusing the old bolts is how clutch jobs come undone.

parts.name: Flywheel
flavorText: Heat crack running through the ring gear area. This can't be machined away — replace it.
Someone had it lightened to 5.5kg. Great for revving, not so great for street driveability.
Face is in resurfaceable condition if the crack isn't through the friction area. Measure before you condemn.

parts.name: CV Axle
flavorText: Inner CV boot is torn and the joint's been slinging grease for months. Joint itself is clicking.
Outer boot is intact but the joint has play in it — worn out from age, not from damage.
Splines are worn on the transmission end. It pulls out too easily. Replace the shaft.

parts.name: Differential
flavorText: Whine at 60 MPH that changes with load — pinion bearing is going. It'll let go eventually.
Diff fluid looks like chocolate milk. Water got in through a cracked cover. Bearings are done.
Torsen-style LSD — someone upgraded this car. Rebuild it, don't replace it.

parts.name: Front Brake Pads

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pads are off. Rotors are grooved deeper than your fingernail. You knew, but now you really know.

flavorText: Worn to the metal backing plate on the inboard side. The grinding noise makes total sense now.
One pad fell out when you removed the caliper. It had been rattling around loose in there.
Semi-metallic compound, all the way worn. Braking into hairpin bends would have been interesting.

parts.name: Front Rotors
flavorText: Grooved past minimum thickness spec. Machining would take them below the line — just replace them.
Hard spots on both rotors — blue discoloration from heavy heat. They'll judder under braking.
One rotor has a crack starting from the hat. Non-negotiable. Replace it immediately.

parts.name: Rear Brake Drums
flavorText: Inside of the drum is grooved and glazed — shoes and drum both need replacing.
Drum is out-of-round by 0.4mm. Above spec. On the rear of an AE86, that's enough to feel.
Return springs inside are rusty and one has snapped. Rear brakes have had zero adjustment in years.

parts.name: Brake Master Cylinder
flavorText: Pedal slowly sinks to the floor under steady pressure. Internal bypass — fluid's past the primary cup.
Reservoir is full but there's a wet stain down the firewall. Rear seal is leaking into the booster.
Bore is corroded and pitted. Rebuilding it isn't worth the risk on a brake component.

parts.name: Brake Lines
flavorText: Hard lines have surface rust all over. Tap one with a wrench and flakes come off. Replace all of them.
Flexible hose at the right rear has ballooned — it's bulging instead of transmitting pressure.
Previous owner used compression fittings instead of proper flares. On brake lines. Unbelievable.

parts.name: Front Struts

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Strut out. Lower control arm bushing is completely disintegrated — crumbled rubber the moment you touched it.

flavorText: Bounce test — car keeps bouncing. Dampers are gone. It's been handling like a boat.
Strut mount bearing is seized. Steering has been fighting you on every slow-speed turn.
Rod seals are blown, oil down the side of both units. Damping is basically zero at this point.

parts.name: Tie Rod Ends
flavorText: Both outer ends have play in the ball joint. Grab the wheel at 9 and 3 — it clunks. It all makes sense now.
Inner tie rod has a torn boot — no grease, full of road grit. The joint is worn through.
They're bent. Just bent. Car was hit at some point and nobody bothered to straighten them.

parts.name: Control Arms
flavorText: Bushings are completely dissolved — you can wiggle the arm 10mm in any direction. Alignment is impossible like this.
Ball joint boot is torn and the joint clicks under load. Replace the whole arm — it's cheaper than parts.
One arm is bent from a curb strike. Subtle enough to miss visually, obvious once you put it on an alignment rack.

parts.name: Rear Struts
flavorText: Rear end hops under braking. Dampers are completely shot — no resistance at all on extension.
Corroded shaft has scored the seal and oil is running down the rear wheel well. That's what you smelled.
Someone installed lowering springs with stock dampers. Rebound rate is catastrophic. Fix both.

parts.name: Sway Bar End Links
flavorText: Clunking over bumps that stops when you push down on that corner — classic loose end link.
Bushings are oval from age. The bar's been flopping around loose. Handling has been vague for years.
One link is broken and being held together by the boot. Cheapest fix on the whole suspension.

systems.name: Interior
flavorText: Door cards are cracked, headliner's drooping, and someone installed a massive aftermarket stereo where the center console used to be.
Seats are ripped but the bolster shape is still good. A classic Trueno interior waiting to be restored.
The spirit of the interior is intact. Mostly. The smell is a mystery that may never be solved.

systems.name: Electrical
flavorText: Wiring loom has been patched with electrical tape in six places. Some of the patches are patching other patches.
The pop-up headlight motors work, but one side is slower than the other. Priorities.
Ground straps are corroded. That explains the intermittent instrument cluster blackout and roughly half the other electrical gremlins.

systems.name: Body Panels
flavorText: Surface rust on all four arches and the rocker panels, but the main structure is solid. It's saveable.
Right rear quarter has a crease from a parking lot incident. Previous owner tried to hide it under matching paint. Didn't work.
Panels are straight underneath the grime. Whoever owned this before you at least kept it garaged.

systems.name: Glass
flavorText: Windscreen has a stress crack from the lower left corner. Passenger has been staring at it for miles.
All glass is intact but the rear window defroster has one dead strip running horizontally across the centre.
The T-bar roof seals are dried out and leaking. Interior gets damp in the rain. Not ideal.

systems.name: Trim
flavorText: The Trueno badge on the rear is missing. The one badge you actually want people to see. Gone.
Rubber window seals are cracked and shrunk away from the glass in places. Wind noise at speed is constant.
Most of the trim clips are broken. Everything rattles. It's charming in a horrible way.

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Rough idle with popping on deceleration. Engine surges between 600 and 1100 RPM and gets worse when warm.

free: Idle instability gets worse once the engine reaches operating temperature
A faint hissing sound is audible from the engine bay at idle
clues.multimeter: TPS reads correctly and injector pulse width is even across all four — the lean condition is not coming from the fuel side
clues.compressionTester: Compression is even across all four cylinders at 185 PSI — the head is fine

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Engine overheats at highway speeds. Temperature gauge climbs steadily above the midpoint and keeps going.

free: Heater output is normal at idle — the coolant is circulating
No steam from the exhaust and coolant level is correct in the reservoir
clues.multimeter: Coolant temp sensor reads accurately against a calibrated thermometer — the gauge is telling the truth
clues.compressionTester: No combustion gases detected in the coolant — head gasket is intact

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Clutch slips during hard acceleration above 4000 RPM. Engine RPM flares without a corresponding increase in road speed.

free: Clutch pedal feels normal — not soft, spongy, or low
No burning smell unless you really push it hard uphill
clues.multimeter: No electrical fault codes — this is a purely mechanical issue

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Brake pedal is spongy and sinks slowly under sustained pressure. Car pulls slightly left under hard braking.

free: Brake fluid reservoir is full with no visible drop in level
No visible fluid leaks at any wheel or along the brake lines
clues.multimeter: Brake light switch is functioning correctly — not an electrical issue

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Hard to start when warm. Black smoke from the exhaust on startup, and there is a strong fuel smell.

free: Car starts fine when cold — the problem appears only after reaching operating temperature
Fuel smell is present in the exhaust and occasionally inside the cabin
clues.multimeter: O2 sensor is reading lean — ECU is trying to add fuel in response, not the root cause
clues.compressionTester: Compression is normal across all cylinders — this is a fuel delivery issue, not a mechanical one

firstStartText: The 4A-GE catches on the third crank. RPM swings to 1200... settles at 850. The idle is smooth. She runs.
loreBlurb: The lightweight FR coupe that started a legend. Fifty years from now, people will still know the AE86.
